> Would you mind sharing with me why you want to introduce asyncio 

Well, for starters this project was my sandbox for learning `asyncio`. I wanted to get it working first, and then introduce `asyncio`. I have not gotten around to that yet due to other projects having priority. But one day I will. But there is a chance that it can be done in a compatible manner. I will have to see. The biggest problem is that `asyncio` introduced the new `async`/`away` syntax which does not exist in Python 2. So it will break Python 2 in a fundamental manner. Maybe there are some ways around it. I'll have to see.

But it may take some time until I get around to that, so you are still good 😉 and a fork is not (yet) necessary, and is also something I would like to avoid if possible.

A simple way to explain the appeal to `asyncio` is that it allows you to easily tell the runtime: "Listen, I'm going to do I/O now, so you can do something else while I wait for a response". In essence it is *similar* (but definitely not the same) as parallelisation (threading & co), but it is easier as you don't have to deal with locks. One way of thinking about it is: Using parallelisation, context-switches are imposed by the kernel and you never know when they happen. Using `asyncio` *you* decide when to switch.

It is a really interesting model which is *perfect* for a network library like `puresnmp`. And many applications using it could really benefit from this. Alas, it is Python3 only.

...also, it's probably best to "pass along" this kwarg:

``build_circuit(tor_state, reactor, path, using_guards=False, timeout=None)``

This change doesn't seem right; in AMQP 0-9-1, timestamp is defined as follows per https://www.rabbitmq.com/resources/specs/amqp0-9-1.pdf:

> Time stamps are held in the 64-bit POSIX time_t format with an accuracy of one second. By using 64 bits
we avoid future wraparound issues associated with 31-bit and 32-bit time_t values.

IIRC, this becomes milliseconds in 0-10-0, but is seconds in 0-9-1. This PR divides the value by 1000000, which makes it appear as if the timestamp is neither seconds, nor milliseconds, but in microseconds.

Ok, I see now why this is pretty much a mess: I agree with you that the original code in `unmarshal_primitive()` was problematic: the use of `schema.is_required` did not work if the passed spec was not for a parameter.

The core issue here is that unmarshalling is used both for the request data (server side) as well as the response from the server (client side). Both use `unmarshal_schema_object()`, for request unmarshalling it's called by `unmarshal_param()`. There is a helper function `is_param_spec()` to distinguish between the two.

We can still access the required field even for model properties: this is why we pass in the swagger spec in addition to the object spec when unmarshalling.

It looks like `required` is only correctly handled during validation on the client side, which is all that was really needed previously. I'd say we stick to the minimal change we need for this to work, in order not to make this PR even bigger.

`buffer_size` is decremented inside the inner loop, so `buffer_size > 0` needs to be a condition on the inner loop. The outer loop handles cases where the length of the data passed to feedAudioContent is larger than the available space in the buffer. In that case, we fill the available space in the buffer, process the entire buffer, shift it by one timestep, then continue filling the available space in the buffer.

The following operations do not have a documented Content-MD5 header:

- PutBucketAcl: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketPUTacl.html
- PutBucketLogging: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketPUTlogging.html
- PutBucketNotification: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketGETnotification.html
- PutBucketPolicy: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketPUTpolicy.html
- PutBucketRequestPayment: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTrequestPaymentPUT.html
- PutBucketVersioning: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketPUTVersioningStatus.html
- PutBucketWebsite: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTBucketPUTwebsite.html
- PutObjectAcl: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTObjectPUTacl.html

These operations can, however, take a Content-MD5 header as described in S3's common request headers documentation: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTCommonRequestHeaders.html.

Thing is, you don't need to add a Content-MD5 check for these operations when using SignatureV4 because the message integrity check is built-in to the sigv4 signature. I think this means that we should either not do anything for these operations (other than hide them in docs) or add a new handler that only adds a Content-MD5 header if the signature version is not sigv4.

Adding a new handler would be a new handler and a separate PR I think.

`condition_test` takes an entire reading and is free to check value, status and/or timestamps. You are maybe confused with the case where `condition_or_value` is an actual value as opposed to a generic function :-)

The explicit status check was introduced by yours truly in the katuilib days already. The evidence:

> r12268 | schwardt | 2012-01-24 09:59:41 +0200 (Tue, 24 Jan 2012) | 1 line
> 
> (schwardt, simonr) Added the ability to wait until an arbitrary condition is satisfied on a sensor, in addition to the default condition of the sensor value being equal to a desired value. The differing wait implementations in KATClient and Array are now unified with the same parameters and behaviour. The signal implementation in KATClient has been replaced by a time-based implementation, which is shorter and slightly easier to understand. The timeouts were made more accurate. Both versions now throw exceptions on missing sensors or ones without a strategy, and both respond to a KeyboardInterrupt. The updated signal implementation in KATClient is kept in commented-out form for posterity.
> 

That is why one writes decent commit messages... Unfortunately it was not decent enough to remind me of the need for an explicit status check :-)

I think my thinking was that you always only want to wait on a 'nominal' sensor, and if that is the case you might as well make it explicit instead of forcing a lambda-type check all the time.

Then, in the translation to katcp, the default became `status=None`, so that whole thing went out the door. But nothing much went wrong, so I guess my need for 'nominal'-ness has been misplaced :-) It sort of makes sense, as warn and error states do not invalidate the actual value of the sensor and should still be wait()-able.

The definition of this ordering is not consistent (see below). I will try to find the original paper for a proper reference.

Neurolucida definition:
```
    Order assignment progresses towards the root segment.
    At each node, if **all daughter** segments are assigned the same order, the parent segment is assigned the daughter segments order "+1."
    If the daughter segments are assigned different orders, the largest order is assigned to the parent segment.
```
Wiki definition:
```
    If the node is a leaf (has no children), its Strahler number is one.
    If the node has one child with Strahler number i, and all other children have Strahler numbers less than i, then the Strahler number of the node is i again.
    If the node has **two or more children** with Strahler number i, and no children with greater number, then the Strahler number of the node is i + 1.
```

Actually it was. According to watchdog logs Patroni shut down was taking ~120 seconds and "magic close" wasn't received from the pipe.

120 seconds comes from 60 seconds * 2, where 2 is a default timeout multiplier when we run tests on travis (sometimes travis is very slow).
features/environment.py sends a SIGTERM to Patroni and waits for 120 seconds until it will shut down normally. After timeout it sends a SIGKILL.

Patroni can't send "magic close" if psutil.Process raise an exception. And Patroni also doesn't shut down normally in this case, because one or more of the threads didn't terminated.

I am not sure why do you think the first part is more complex? I wanted to make sure that when you call `assertFieldWorks`, this tests everything for that field. And the different checks that are performed (test simulated operation, test output and test descriptor) are documented in the function. I don't want to see that you need to write two different test functions (or more) for each field, because this means that it is harder to extend and some test cases can be missed when adding a new field.

This makes it easy to extend test cases:
* When adding a new field, one just needs to call `self.assertFieldWorks` with the correct parameters.
* When extending tests for each field, one just needs to modify the `assertFieldWorks` to include additional checks for each field, without the need to modify all the tests.

> If you set the Content instance's get_bytes to full_log.readlines, then it
> gets to iterate over it once and then will be at EOF if it tries to do it

> to use an Equals matcher on it: it fails the match, then tries to iterate
> over the content again to construct the failure message and you just get

The targeted distros (debian, rhel, suse) all use systemd.

Rather than trying to detect if `systemd-cron` or `systemd-cron-next` are installed, I think it would be better to detect if `systemd` is the init. If it is, then create a `certbot-renew.timer` and a `certbot-renew.unit` and don't do anything with cron. This will work on _any_ distro using systemd (archlinux, ubuntu, fedora, mint, etc, etc, etc), and then the cron editing can be used for legacy systems and systems that don't ship systemd (gentoo, the BSDs, etc).

I recommend not to modify let variable which is passed a a parameter, but do the masking on the result, like:

```
>>> b = np.asarray( [1.0, 1.0] )
>>> a = np.asarray( [1.0, 0.0] )
>>> b/a
array([  1.,  inf])
>>> c = b/a
>>> c
array([  1.,  inf])
>>> c[c==np.inf] = 100
>>> c
array([   1.,  100.])
```

This would require:
```
result = (1 - np.exp(-let/ld) * (1 + let/ld)) * ld/let
```
